opp.ais_why:
• On the 3/25/2026 call, Tony Singleton was unambiguous about deprioritizing AI intake: the majority of users will bypass Merlin Intake and go straight to eProcurement, with only roughly 2% of occasional business users as Merlin candidates. He framed AI intake as useful only for the occasional non-procurement user, not for the 98% who buy from catalog.
• Tony also voiced a direct quality objection on the demo, saying the perceived AI slowness raised concerns about responsiveness, and noted that a competitor had demoed faster direct quote upload inside eProcurement. The buyer is actively benchmarking against AI alternatives and finds Zycus's flow slower today.
• The RFP itself is rated "No AI Requirement." The scope is catalog management, punch-outs to Grainger and CDW, ERP receipt flow, and rate cards: classic rules-based eProcurement. No AI POC, no AI-tied budget, no AI champion.

metrics.narrative: The business problem is clearly articulated but not yet quantified. Tony Singleton (02 Mar 2026) said procurement in the Americas 'is very immature... we haven't really invested much from a technology point of view' and processes are 'very manual today.' On 03 Mar he described the current requisition-to-PO flow as users manually re-keying orders from Grainger into VAI with email approvals, calling it 'horrible.' He flagged ~80% spend-data accuracy as a problem (03 Mar). Crucially, on 23 Feb he stated he lacks baseline data: 'I don't have any data which tells them how many requisitions are being raised, how long it takes for them to convert to a PO... how much that's costing,' and committed to gather it to build the business case. No ROI, savings, headcount, or cycle-time numbers have been produced. The ranked move is to co-build the baseline/ROI model with Tony so the value case is quantified for leadership.

champion.narrative: Tony Singleton (US Procurement Manager) is a developing champion: he is driving the evaluation, openly sharing his strategy, internal constraints, and competitor activity, and brought colleagues (a 'Mike' and additional buyers) into the 10 Mar and 25 Mar demos. He is engaged and responsive (last activity 22 Jun). What has developed: active discovery, multi-attendee demos, willingness to co-build a business case. What is still missing: Tony is an evaluator without budget authority and must align upward with the unnamed global Procurement Excellence Director, so he cannot yet carry the deal to signature alone. He has not yet introduced the economic buyer or the global stakeholder into the Zycus evaluation. Securing Tony as a fully empowered champion means equipping him with the ROI case and getting him to open the door to power.

competition.narrative: This is a competitive, multi-vendor evaluation. Salesforce Competitors lists Coupa, GEP Worldwide, JAGGAER, and SAP Ariba, and Tony confirmed on 25 Mar he is evaluating '4-5 solution providers.' On 23 Feb he noted he 'just spoke to another provider offering something similar recently. They've given me a few... live demos... We've had some initial pricing from from them as well,' showing at least one rival is ahead of Zycus on pricing and demos. SAP Ariba is a latent strategic threat because AAK's global team is on SAP and plans a SAP migration in 3-5 years, which could favor a native SAP path. Zycus's wedge is the Microsoft Teams-embedded Merlin Intake guided-buying experience for low change-management overhead, plus the modular ability to start with eProcurement only and integrate with VAI now and SAP later. The named rival's identity on 23 Feb was not stated, so per-vendor ranking is partly inferred; the move is to confirm the competitive shortlist in the RFP.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is specific and confirmed by the buyer. Tony Singleton (03 Mar 2026) described users finding items on the Grainger site, then manually re-keying requisitions into VAI with no system connection, layered email approvals, and an all-or-nothing dollar-threshold approval flow in the ERP: 'There's a lot of steps... It's horrible.' Rogue/maverick spend is a core pain: 'we have no way of kind of steering the business' to preferred suppliers (03 Mar). Spend data is only ~80% accurate because POs often lack part numbers or descriptions, blocking sub-category analysis (03 Mar). The pain owner is Tony's lean US procurement team, which risks becoming a bottleneck (his words, 03 Mar). This is a real, owned operational pain, though not yet costed.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are essentially unknown. Justin's joint evaluation plan (03 Mar) lists implementation scoping, finalized contract draft, and execution as the closing steps, but Tony said those are 'a little bit further down the line' and 'very much exploratory.' No MSA/SOW, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement channel has been discussed. A future SAP migration (3-5 years) and the question of professional-services charges for re-platforming were raised on 25 Mar but not formalized. The forcing function that would create paper-process visibility is the RFP and a quantified business case; until the business case clears Tony's leadership, no contracting will start.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has been identified or engaged, and the approval chain for the $75k purchase is undefined. Tony Singleton is the evaluator and US procurement manager driving the deal but does not control budget; on 02 Mar he said he is 'part of our leadership team for the globally for procurement' yet must 'align with them on this is what I'm looking to do.' On 23 Feb (an earlier, separate AAK touchpoint) Claudia Maria Torres Guzman pointed to a procurement lead named Kevin as the decision-maker for sourcing, but Kevin has not appeared on any eProcurement evaluation call. The global Procurement Excellence Director (not named) is referenced as engaged only for later-stage global rollout (25 Mar). The evidence shows budget authority sits above Tony and is unmapped; the ranked move is to have Tony name and introduce the budget holder for the $75k investment.

decision_process.narrative: The process is readable from the call sequence and the joint evaluation plan Justin shared on 03 Mar: intro call (23 Feb) -> discovery (02-03 Mar) -> demo (10 Mar, Tony added colleagues including a 'Mike') -> preliminary pricing (13 Mar, not recorded) -> deeper eProcurement demo (25 Mar) -> RFP. Tony confirmed (25 Mar) he is evaluating 4-5 vendors and is still in discovery, gating progress on (a) collecting baseline data for the business case and (b) aligning with the global procurement excellence team whose own eProcurement plan sits in the 2027-2030 roadmap. The SF Next Step (JRA 5/12) shows Zycus has been notified it is shortlisted and offered to help author the requirements document, meaning a formal RFP is the next gate but has not yet been issued. The approval chain above Tony is still undefined.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are well documented from the discovery and demo calls. Tony Singleton's top-3 day-one priorities (03 Mar 2026) are: (1) channel the business to preferred suppliers to stop rogue spend, (2) improve policy compliance, and (3) improve spend-data accuracy beyond the current ~80%. He scoped the primary use case as eProcurement intake / guided buying inside Microsoft Teams for physical goods (MRO, office supplies, lab equipment) with interest in services too (23 Feb). Integration is a hard criterion: on 25 Mar he required bidirectional sync with the VAI ERP (PO, receipt, invoice) and the existing CLM, plus the ability to migrate to SAP within 3-5 years without re-implementation. A specific feature test emerged on 25 Mar: can service rate cards be uploaded and managed as catalog items (Ritika could not confirm live). These criteria are explicit and should anchor the demo/RFP positioning.

hard.ais_why:
• On the 3/25/2026 call, Tony Singleton was unambiguous about deprioritizing AI intake: the majority of users will bypass Merlin Intake and go straight to eProcurement, with only roughly 2% of occasional business users as Merlin candidates. He framed AI intake as useful only for the occasional non-procurement user, not for the 98% who buy from catalog.
• Tony also voiced a direct quality objection on the demo, saying the perceived AI slowness raised concerns about responsiveness, and noted that a competitor had demoed faster direct quote upload inside eProcurement. The buyer is actively benchmarking against AI alternatives and finds Zycus's flow slower today.
• The RFP itself is rated "No AI Requirement." The scope is catalog management, punch-outs to Grainger and CDW, ERP receipt flow, and rate cards: classic rules-based eProcurement. No AI POC, no AI-tied budget, no AI champion.

deltas.subject: The 27 Aug 2026 close date is optimistic given AAK is still in discovery across 4-5 vendors and has not yet issued the RFP, down-selected to finalists, or begun contract negotiation. Tony stated on 2 Mar that he is still gathering baseline data to build the business case, and on 25 Mar he confirmed he is 'in discovery phase.' An RFP round, vendor selection, commercials, MSA/SOW, legal/InfoSec review, and ERP integration scoping cannot realistically complete by late August. The close date will slip to Q4 2026 or early 2027, and if Zycus does not manage Tony's expectations and re-anchor the timeline, it could create forecast misalignment and pressure the sales team to discount or rush the deal.

deltas.subject: Service rate-card management capability gap. deltas.subject: Map the Economic Buyer and secure executive sponsorship. Tony Singleton (Procurement Manager, Evaluator) is a strong champion but does not control the $75k budget. No EB or Executive Sponsor has been identified or engaged in any call. The global procurement excellence director (mentioned by Tony on 25 Mar as being engaged for 'later-stage global rollout') has not been named or brought into the process. Nigel Glover (Director of USA Engineering and Operations, a contact role) has not appeared on any call. The next move is to ask Tony directly: 'Who approves the budget for this investment, and when can we brief them on the ROI?' Then schedule an executive connect (with the deal owner's manager or a Zycus senior leader) to present the business case and build EB sponsorship. This is the single highest-leverage move to de-risk the deal and advance it from Pipeline to Best Case.

deltas.to: Help Tony build the quantified business case and position Zycus as the trusted advisor. Tony stated on 2 Mar that he is still gathering baseline data (requisiti
deltas.subject: Help Tony build the quantified business case and position Zycus as the trusted advisor. Tony stated on 2 Mar that he is still gathering baseline data (requisition volumes, cycle times, costs) and that 'without being able to get my baseline data in order first, I'm not gonna be able to sell it to anybody internally.' Zycus should proactively provide a standard ROI template or co-author a business-case deck with Tony, using benchmark data from similar eProcurement deployments (e.g. 40-60% reduction in req-to-PO cycle time, 20-30% tail-spend capture, 0.5-1 FTE savings per 1,000 requisitions). This will accelerate Tony's internal approval process, differentiate Zycus from the 4-5 competing vendors (who likely have not offered this level of support), and position Zycus as a strategic partner rather than just a vendor. Justin offered to help Tony draft the RFP requirements (~12 May NextStep), which is a good start, but the business-case help is even more critical and should be delivered in the next 2 weeks.

deltas.subject: Advance past the RFP round and multi-thread into the buying committee. AAK is evaluating 4-5 vendors (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER, SAP Ariba confirmed in SF Competitors__c, plus possibly one more). Tony has not indicated a preference, and no finalist or down-select has occurred. Zycus's wedge is Merlin Intake's conversational AI in Teams (which impressed Tony and colleagues on the demos) and integration flexibility for the VAI-to-SAP migration journey. The next moves are: (1) Offer a pilot or sandbox environment so Tony and his team can test Merlin Intake and eProcurement with real AAK users and use cases before the RFP decision—this will create stickiness and proof of value that competing vendors cannot match. (2) Multi-thread by bringing Nigel Glover (Director of USA Engineering and Operations, contact role) and the global procurement excellence director into a use-case or ROI session to build broader sponsorship and reduce single-champion risk. (3) Reinforce the Merlin + integration differentiation in all communications and position Zycus as the only vendor that can deliver immediate ROI on VAI today AND seamless SAP migration in 3-5 years without re-implementation.

value.flag: Help Tony build the quantified business case and position Zycus as the trusted advisor. Tony stated on 2 Mar that he is still gathering baseline data (requisition volumes, cycle times, costs) and that 'without being able to get my baseline data in order first, I'm not gonna be able to sell it to anybody internally.' Zycus should proactively provide a standard ROI template or co-author a business-case deck with Tony, using benchmark data from similar eProcurement deployments (e.g. 40-60% reduction in req-to-PO cycle time, 20-30% tail-spend capture, 0.5-1 FTE savings per 1,000 requisitions). This will accelerate Tony's internal approval process, differentiate Zycus from the 4-5 competing vendors (who likely have not offered this level of support), and position Zycus as a strategic partner rather than just a vendor. Justin offered to help Tony draft the RFP requirements (~12 May NextStep), which is a good start, but the business-case help is even more critical and should be delivered in the next 2 weeks.

value.flag: Advance past the RFP round and multi-thread into the buying committee. AAK is evaluating 4-5 vendors (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER, SAP Ariba confirmed in SF Competitors__c, plus possibly one more). Tony has not indicated a preference, and no finalist or down-select has occurred. Zycus's wedge is Merlin Intake's conversational AI in Teams (which impressed Tony and colleagues on the demos) and integration flexibility for the VAI-to-SAP migration journey. The next moves are: (1) Offer a pilot or sandbox environment so Tony and his team can test Merlin Intake and eProcurement with real AAK users and use cases before the RFP decision—this will create stickiness and proof of value that competing vendors cannot match. (2) Multi-thread by bringing Nigel Glover (Director of USA Engineering and Operations, contact role) and the global procurement excellence director into a use-case or ROI session to build broader sponsorship and reduce single-champion risk. (3) Reinforce the Merlin + integration differentiation in all communications and position Zycus as the only vendor that can deliver immediate ROI on VAI today AND seamless SAP migration in 3-5 years without re-implementation.
